tldr: GitHub Actions runs full CI/CD; Depot accelerates Docker image builds. A direct switch only makes sense if your dominant pain is image build time. More commonly, you keep Actions and call Depot from inside it for builds.
why_switch: Faster, cached Docker builds via remote builders
Reduces Actions runner minutes spent on image builds
what_to_export: Workflow steps that run docker build/buildx
Cache mounts and layer caching configs
Workflow secrets used by image build
export_method: Identify which workflow steps actually build container images. Pull the Dockerfiles and any helper scripts from your repo. List build args, secrets (by name), and platform flags currently passed to docker buildx. Note any registry-specific authentication actions you use (login-action variants). Capture the matrix of architectures you build for. Catalog any cache strategies in use (gha cache, registry cache). Secrets stay where they are in GitHub Actions; you do not export their values, only know which ones need to be referenced from the new step.
import_method: Install the Depot CLI in your workflow (or use the official depot-build-push GitHub Action). Replace docker buildx build invocations with depot build, passing your existing Dockerfile, context, build args, platforms, and tags. Authenticate to your registry as before, push images the same way. Configure Depot project IDs and tokens via repo or org-level Actions secrets. Validate that produced images match the previous build's digest and pass downstream tests, then promote to production workflows. Keep all your other Actions jobs (tests, deploy, release) unchanged.

concept_mapping.target: Same secret, referenced from depot step

effort: Hours to a few days for a single repo; up to a week or two if you operate many image build pipelines.
gotchas: Depot does not replace Actions, you still need workflows around it
Verify image digests and tests after each migrated pipeline
Multi-arch builds need explicit platform flags
Secret-mount syntax differs from buildx in some cases
Confirm pricing fits your build volume before cutting over

email.subject: Adopting Depot for container builds inside GitHub Actions
email.body:
Hi Depot team,

We are integrating Depot into our GitHub Actions workflows to speed up container image builds. The plan over the next 14 days is to swap docker buildx for depot build in a couple of pilot repos, validate digest parity and downstream tests, then roll it out across the rest of our image-building pipelines.

We will keep GitHub Actions as our orchestration layer; Depot will only handle the image build step. We expect this to reduce our Actions runner minutes meaningfully.

If you have a recommended onboarding sequence or a preferred way to size our plan based on weekly build volume, we would appreciate the guidance. Otherwise we will start on the entry tier and adjust.

Thanks,
Your customer
